summary: McKenzie shares lessons from selling low-cost software ($24.95) to non-technical users who don't typically buy software — people whose computer use is limited to email, documents, and eBay. He argues that visual polish matters far more than features for this audience, that personal accessibility and responsive customer support are essential for converting hesitant buyers, and that the purchasing process must be streamlined to near-foolproof simplicity. The post challenges the common uISV assumption that the target market is other developers.
key_points: 80% of his customers don't identify as software buyers — they use computers for basic tasks and don't seek out new applications
Visual polish drives more positive customer response than any feature addition, even though no one explicitly asks for better aesthetics
Skittish non-technical customers often send a single support email as a trust test before purchasing — proving a real person is behind the product
The ordering pathway must be failure-proof: minimal options, no unnecessary fields, and every click should lead toward purchase
Selling to non-developers avoids the OSS competition and 'I could build that myself' mentality of developer customers
money_quotes: Roughly 80% of my customers do not buy software. I don't mean this in the sense that they are pirates. I mean this in the sense that their relationship to their computer is not primarily mediated through applications.
Do you know how many people ever mailed me saying 'Your program looks kind of drab'? Not a single one.

Some people say that if you're selling at my price point ($24.95) you cannot afford to offer personalized customer support. I say you can't afford not to: your skittish prospective customers seeking reassurance will run away.
My theory on this is that people want to know that the program is being actively maintained by a real live human being, and they want to know that if there is a problem somebody will be on the case.
You'll be forever worrying about OSS coming along and eating your lunch, and your customers are in general loathe to spend money to accomplish something they think they can just whip up themselves.
Practically anything you click within the main body of the page will take you directly to the ordering page.
key_insight: Selling software to non-technical users requires prioritizing visual polish, personal accessibility, and a frictionless purchase path over features — because these customers are buying trust and reassurance, not capabilities.
